purpose: HUMAN ADJUDICATION ONLY. These rows had unrepresentative quotes AND the transcript shows the labeled direction is opposite or absent. NO automated mutation was applied. Nimrod decides whether to keep, re-mark unresolved, or flip — flipping changes scoring and is a human-only decision.

rows.judge_why: Host already owns FICO shares, has a buy price, and recommends buying dips — clearly bullish.
rows.conflicting_transcript_sentence: So, you're going to have to take those opportunities when FICO does dip seriously and buy when that happens cuz it's not going to last for long with this business.
rows.currently_displayed_quote: So now to get back to my buy price of $1,340, they would have to fall about 13.4%. And FICO did fall because there's a lot of trepidation in the market around an alternative to their highly monopolistic FICO score. It's called the Vantage Score 4.0. Again, check out that video for what that means to FICO's business. It's essentially an alternative scoring method for lenders to use to score mortgage borrowers on their ability to repay their loans. I explained in that video why it won't affect fair Isaac the way the market currently believes it will. And my thesis there is that the alternative scoring method will become something like what we see with S&P Global and Moody's where lenders will want to use multiple scores to assess a borrower's potential to repay their loans. And that would only strengthen FICO's position in the market. In all honesty though, my FICO buy price of $1,340 is about a 5% margin of safety.

rows.judge_why: Host explicitly says Nike has no competitive advantage; bullish view belongs to Aman.
rows.conflicting_transcript_sentence: Personally, I do not see the competitive advantage that Nike has anymore.
rows.currently_displayed_quote: Well, Aman sold 100% of his Nike shares, but what he actually did was convert that into call options instead. Aman still believes Nike is a turnaround play. Aman said that Nike has made three mistakes which has caused erosion of shareholder value. Number one, they switched to focusing on a direct to consumer strategy at the expense of wholesale partnerships. Number two, a merchandising structure that did not prioritize sports. And number three, overproduction of bestselling franchises in place of working harder on innovation. The third is best summed up as resting on their laurels. What all of that actually means is Nike is not focusing on the core business that made them great. His rationale for converting to options instead was this, and I quote, "In a successful turnaround, the option payoffs should be more than double the returns from owning the common stock." We continue to believe that Nike has the potential to be one of the great large cap consumer turnarounds.
